rationale: The cited paper is used for more than background listing: it is explicitly invoked to define mental imagery as a cognitive process and to anchor prior evidence that visualization affects evaluations. These points are tied to the citing paper's theorizing about cognitive load and imagery-based effects on rescue-based food demand. Although the citing article does not reuse the cited study's exact product context, it positions its core contribution as moving that mental-imagery line into a new domain and valence.
relation_description: The citing paper builds on Zhao, Hoeffler, and Dahl (2009) as prior mental-imagery research showing evaluation effects, then extends that logic to rescue-based food. It argues that imagery can also harm evaluations when marketing cues evoke physical-waste associations and disgust.
